title: Chicago, 1893: Shadows of the White City
description: A 20-minute historical thriller set during the World's Columbian Exposition. A conspiracy threatens to sabotage the fair's opening night lighting demonstration.

pressureEvents: A telegraph arrives with contradictory instructions
A guard reports movement near restricted electrical equipment
A newspaper man gets wind of a scandal

winConditions: The true sabotage method is identified
The player prevents or neutralizes the sabotage
Burnham or fair security accepts the player's warning in time
failConditions: Time expires before action is taken
The player accuses the wrong person and loses critical support
The sabotage succeeds sufficiently to damage the fair's opening or reputation
partialSuccessExamples: The immediate sabotage is stopped, but the conspirators escape
The fair opens safely, but scandal reaches the newspapers
Burnham is saved political embarrassment, but the player sacrifices an ally

categories.name: White City Construction & Fair Administration Argot
categories.context: Used by Burnham, his engineers, and senior fair staff when discussing logistics, infrastructure, and the exposition's internal operations.

terms.meaning: The Court of Honor, the central lagoon plaza — the symbolic and operational heart of the exposition; shorthand for anything of highest priority or visibility.
Construction using plaster mixed with jute fiber to create the fair's iconic white facades — 'staff' refers both to the material and to any rushed, surface-level fix meant to look permanent.
Burnham's master construction and event timetable, treated as near-sacred; to 'break the Schedule' is a serious professional offense.
Any electrical line or zone that has lost power or been deliberately disconnected; also used metaphorically for a department or person cut out of communications.
Engineers and technical exhibitors centered in Machinery Hall — considered pragmatic, unglamorous, and politically reliable compared to the fine-arts faction.
A directive issued with absolute authority and no expectation of debate; invoking Burnham's name directly to end an argument or commandeer resources.
Any logistical or structural issue caused by the exposition's proximity to Lake Michigan — flooding, wind, unstable ground — used broadly for any crisis with no clean solution.

categories.name: Chicago Ward Politics & Fixer Cant
categories.context: Used by Hanrahan and his network of aldermen, precinct captains, and procurement contacts when conducting informal business around the fair.

terms.meaning: The informal alliance of aldermen and ward bosses who control city contracts and patronage — to 'go through the combine' means to pay the appropriate political toll.
A bribe or kickback, always spoken of in polite, euphemistic terms; the amount is never named aloud in a first conversation.
Money paid for political favors or graft; a 'boodler' is an alderman known to accept such payments, which in 1893 Chicago describes most of them.
The corrupt faction of the Chicago City Council notorious for selling franchises and contracts; Hanrahan's primary institutional connection.
To pay off or otherwise neutralize a beat cop or low-level official who might interfere with an arrangement.
A contract or task handled entirely off the books, with no paper trail — named for the practice of disposing of inconvenient records in the Chicago River.
A debt of favor owed to Hanrahan or the ward; someone 'on the slate' is expected to deliver a service when called upon, no questions asked.
Tacit approval from ward-level political authority — without it, no significant business in a given neighborhood proceeds safely.

categories.name: Electrical Trade Shorthand
categories.context: Used by Mercier and the fair's electrical engineers when discussing the Tesla-Westinghouse AC lighting system powering the exposition's opening-night demonstration.

terms.meaning: The Westinghouse AC system powering the fair — always referred to with a note of professional pride by its supporters and barely concealed contempt by Edison partisans.
The twelve Westinghouse generators in the Machinery Hall that produce the fair's electrical supply; the physical and symbolic core of the lighting demonstration.
An unintended electrical connection to earth that can disrupt or short an entire circuit — the most plausible cover story for a deliberate act of sabotage.
The central control panel governing power distribution across the fairgrounds; access to it is tightly restricted and its location is not on public maps.
The unit of electrical frequency, newly named for Heinrich Hertz — Mercier uses it casually as a marker of technical sophistication; American engineers sometimes still say 'cycles.'
A conductor carrying no current, either by design or failure — used figuratively by Mercier to describe a contact or lead that has gone silent or unreliable.
The total electrical demand of the fairgrounds at any given moment; 'carrying the load' on opening night is the central engineering challenge and point of vulnerability.

categories.name: Press & Society Reporter's Vernacular
categories.context: Used by Clara Hale and her journalistic peers when discussing sources, stories, and the social intelligence network surrounding the exposition.

terms.meaning: A newspaper's archive of clippings and reference files — Clara's primary research tool and a source of leverage when she needs background on a suspicious figure.
A freelance correspondent paid by the column inch — Clara's network of stringers feeds her gossip from hotels, restaurants, and exposition offices she cannot access directly.
Information too polite or too dangerous to print as hard news — used ironically to describe rumors with serious implications that no editor will touch without ironclad sourcing.
The informal circuit of exposition-adjacent hotels — the Palmer House, the Auditorium — where Clara cultivates sources among visiting dignitaries, exhibitors, and their staff.
An exclusive story obtained before rival papers — in 1893 Chicago journalism, obtaining one sometimes requires trading favors as much as conducting interviews.
Information given in confidence, not for direct attribution — Clara uses this agreement strategically, sometimes honoring it and sometimes finding creative ways around it.
The telegraph news services — Associated Press and United Press — whose correspondents Clara both competes with and occasionally feeds selective information to shape a story's national framing.

categories.name: Fair Security & Chicago Police Cant
categories.context: Used by O'Donnell and the Columbian Guard officers when coordinating patrols, reporting incidents, and navigating the uneasy boundary between fair jurisdiction and city police authority.

terms.meaning: The Columbian Guard, the exposition's private security force — distinct from the Chicago Police Department and a frequent source of jurisdictional friction with it.
An unidentified body found in the lagoon or on fairgrounds — a recurring and politically sensitive problem given the exposition's international press coverage.
The police wagon used to transport arrested persons — its appearance on fairgrounds is considered a public-relations disaster to be avoided at nearly any cost.
Receiving regular graft payments from a fixed source — O'Donnell uses this to identify which city officers can be trusted to act independently and which cannot.
Covert surveillance of a suspect or area, conducted without the subject's knowledge — authorized only by O'Donnell personally and kept off the official duty roster.

The Pinkerton National Detective Agency, whose operatives are sometimes hired for sensitive exposition security matters that O'Donnell cannot handle through official channels — a professional rivalry he resents.
To lock down a section of the fairgrounds and restrict movement — a drastic measure O'Donnell resists using because it draws press attention and requires Burnham's authorization.
